title: Everything Sunny All The Time Always

jokes.text: Hush, island baby / Tomorrow you'll shuck the cane / Your little fingers bloody / Prepare to be whipped
jokes.punchline: Turns out to be about child labor and violence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - starts as tender lullaby, becomes horrifying child labor song. Perfect Jack character moment showing his obliviousness to normalcy.

jokes.text: No, when she's ready, Dr. Kevorkian says we have to put her down. He's a very good pediatrician, but that is an unfortunate name.
jokes.setup: Discussion of baby care with doctor recommendation
jokes.punchline: Doctor shares name with famous assisted suicide advocate
jokes.explanation: Classic 30 Rock dark wordplay. The casual delivery of 'put her down' followed by acknowledgment of the unfortunate name is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: No. Call back tomorrow. She has Babynomics at 11:00.
jokes.punchline: Baby has economics class
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack parenting - treating infant like a tiny executive. The portmanteau 'Babynomics' is clever and character-specific.

jokes.text: What are you doing? That's his urine. He is not well.
jokes.setup: Liz about to drink what she thinks is soda
jokes.punchline: It's Tracy's urine
jokes.explanation: Effective gross-out comedy that reveals Tracy's deteriorating mental state. Kenneth's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Conrad Bain once slapped me in a men's room.
jokes.setup: Discussion of making apartment into duplex
jokes.punchline: Non sequitur about celebrity encounter
jokes.explanation: Classic Kenneth random backstory drop. Works because it's so unexpectedly specific and delivered casually.

jokes.text: Comencé a estudiar these, but then yo gave up.
jokes.punchline: Mixes English and Spanish incorrectly
jokes.explanation: Clever linguistic joke showing Kenneth's failed attempt at bilingualism. The 'yo gave up' is perfectly wrong.

jokes.text: Liz stabbed Jenna repeatedly.
jokes.punchline: First line is about murdering Jenna
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical dark humor revealing Liz's true feelings about Jenna. The casual delivery makes it brilliant.

jokes.text: You saw how flat Gwyneth Paltrow sang at the Oscars. I visualized all of that.
jokes.punchline: Takes credit for another actress's failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna vanity - using mystical powers to sabotage other actresses. Very character-specific narcissism.

jokes.text: Well, I've taken Action. It dries your mouth out, but the sex is amazing.
jokes.punchline: Jenna thinks she means the drug Action
jokes.explanation: Classic misunderstanding joke. Jenna's immediate assumption it's about drugs is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I always think of a third thing when I'm listing stuff.
jokes.punchline: Meta-commentary on the rule of three
jokes.explanation: Self-aware writing joke about comedy structure. Meta but not too clever for its own good.

jokes.text: I've got a new life philosophy that I call 'Lizbeanism.' Well, I'm Liz, and obviously, my philosophy is simple, like a bean.
jokes.punchline: Explanation reveals it sounds like lesbianism but she means beans
jokes.explanation: Great accidental double entendre. Liz's complete obliviousness to the sexual connotation is perfectly her.

jokes.text: Because Lizbeanism means that I am a dike... against the rising waters of mediocrity.
jokes.punchline: Another accidental lesbian reference, saved by civil engineering metaphor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the accidental lesbian theme. The pause before 'against the rising waters' is comedy gold.

jokes.text: I'm going to give you... one of my neckties.
jokes.setup: Jack is impressed with Liz's new attitude
jokes.punchline: His gesture of respect is giving her masculine clothing
jokes.explanation: Jack's paternalistic gesture of giving Liz masculine clothing to show respect is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Wait, who was the white guy in that?
jokes.punchline: Liz immediately thinks of the movie instead of the poem
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Liz's pop culture focus vs Jack's classical education.

jokes.text: having people pay you for the privilege of cutting your hair.
jokes.punchline: Bizarre flex about paying for haircuts
jokes.explanation: Absurd rich person flex that's perfectly Jack. The idea of paying for the 'privilege' is wonderfully pretentious.

jokes.text: 'Hot Blondes in Weird Places' initiative
jokes.punchline: NBC News has a program with this ridiculous name
jokes.explanation: Savage satire of cable news. Perfectly captures the absurdity of modern media in one phrase.

jokes.text: I wish Liz Lemon would leave so I could go back to work. Oh! Ugh! What's pulling me? I want to stay here and keep boring Jack!
jokes.setup: Jenna wishing via The Secret for Liz to leave
jokes.punchline: Liz gets physically pulled away while protesting
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with meta element about The Secret actually working. Good escalation from setup.

jokes.text: Great impression of a guy that sucks, Dotcom!
jokes.punchline: Tracy insults him for being welcoming
jokes.explanation: Classic Tracy meanness disguised as a compliment. Shows his narcissistic need to dominate his entourage.

jokes.text: Smooth move, Ferguson.
jokes.punchline: Generic sarcastic response becomes running gag
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's running gag. Not particularly funny on its own but becomes important to the plot.

jokes.text: I could have inside jokes that you're not a part of, for example, 'Hot feet' or 'Ask Melissa about it.'
jokes.setup: Tracy asserting dominance over inside jokes
jokes.punchline: Makes up completely random non-jokes
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Tracy's childish logic and desperate need for control. The random phrases are beautifully nonsensical.

jokes.text: You wouldn't expect a movie called 'Somewhere' to go absolutely nowhere.
jokes.punchline: Title pun about going nowhere
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay on the Sofia Coppola film. Simple but effective structure.

jokes.text: Sometimes we use a song to move a story along / And explain it to you / 'Cause Liz is taking charge
jokes.punchline: Song explicitly explains its own narrative function
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on musical exposition. Self-aware about its own artificiality.

jokes.text: Who's ready for Skype sex? No, no, no. This is the Liddy call.
jokes.punchline: Sexual proposition during baby call
jokes.explanation: Good awkward moment showing the couple's confusion about video calls and inappropriate timing.

jokes.text: I don't know why our daughter would be afraid of Reagan.
jokes.punchline: Baby is afraid of Ronald Reagan (presumably a photo/poster)
jokes.explanation: Perfect character detail - conservative couple has Reagan imagery that scares their infant.

jokes.text: You people have too much money.
jokes.punchline: Jack, who is extremely wealthy, complains about rich people
jokes.explanation: Jack's complete lack of self-awareness about his wealth. Ironic coming from him specifically.

jokes.text: You people have too much money.
jokes.punchline: Avery's matter-of-fact assessment of their wealth during tech problems
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective commentary on their privilege delivered in character.

jokes.text: Bring back some throwing stars for Liddy
jokes.setup: Jack telling Avery to stay longer in Asia
jokes.punchline: Casually requests ninja weapons as baby gifts
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack parenting philosophy - treating baby like tiny executive. Absurd but delivered matter-of-factly.

jokes.text: Do you want to watch me eat jelly beans... real slow? Yes.
jokes.setup: Couple trying to be intimate over video
jokes.punchline: Their idea of sexting is watching slow jelly bean consumption
jokes.explanation: Hilariously unsexy attempt at phone sex. Perfect for their uptight corporate personalities.

jokes.text: and the delivery guy tripped, and then Mr. Griswald said, 'Smooth move, Ferguson.'
jokes.setup: Explaining the origin of 'Smooth move, Ferguson'
jokes.punchline: Completely mundane, unfunny origin story
jokes.explanation: The anticlimatic explanation is the joke - builds up to nothing, which is why Tracy doesn't get it.

jokes.text: That story is not funny. Sir, you had to be there. Then I will be.
jokes.punchline: Tracy decides to recreate the entire event
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy logic - if he had to be there to understand, he'll literally be there. Shows his obsessive narcissism brilliantly.

jokes.text: People of North Korea, it is your dear leader, Kim Jong-il, world's greatest golfer and movie director and best man at Tom Brady's wedding.
jokes.punchline: Absurd false claims including Tom Brady wedding
jokes.explanation: Good satire of North Korean propaganda with absurd American cultural references. The Tom Brady detail is perfect randomness.

jokes.text: because she can't party as hard as we can.
jokes.punchline: North Korea described as party destination
jokes.explanation: Absurd reframing of North Korean oppression as being too fun. Good contrast with reality.

jokes.text: because it's awesome and we have enough food.
jokes.setup: Why another reporter came to North Korea
jokes.punchline: Claims about having food (notorious for famine)
jokes.explanation: Dark irony about North Korean food shortages. Simple but effective political satire.

jokes.text: Sir, if you say one more syllable, that's a haiku.
jokes.setup: Jack expressing anguish about his wife
jokes.punchline: Jonathan notices he's accidentally forming a haiku
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke that breaks the fourth wall on accidental poetry. Shows Jonathan's bizarre attention to detail during crisis.

jokes.text: Don't go in there! Run, citizen!
jokes.punchline: Someone warns her away like it's dangerous
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag treating bureaucracy like a horror movie. Sets up Liz's bureaucratic nightmare.

jokes.text: President InterBush is out of the question.
jokes.punchline: Calls Clinton 'President InterBush' (between Bushes)
jokes.explanation: Clever political joke reducing Clinton to a Bush interruption. Very Jack-like Republican dismissiveness.

jokes.text: 'Me plus you equals frowny face.'
jokes.setup: Admitting he broke up with Condoleezza Rice by text
jokes.punchline: The actual childish breakup text
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy - sophisticated Jack reducing relationship to emoji math. The equation format is hilariously clinical.

jokes.text: Use it to break into a special-effects warehouse to steal one.
jokes.punchline: Tracy immediately suggests criminal activity
jokes.explanation: Classic Tracy escalation - goes straight to felony for minor inconvenience. Shows his detachment from normal solutions.

jokes.text: Dotcom licks the subway steps.
jokes.punchline: Grotesque reason for illness
jokes.explanation: Effective gross-out humor. Kenneth's matter-of-fact delivery of disgusting information works well.

jokes.text: Do you have access to horse semen?
jokes.punchline: Casual question about horse semen
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna moment - casually discussing bizarre beauty treatments. The nonchalant delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Dotcom licks the subway steps.
jokes.setup: Kenneth explaining they can't recreate Dotcom being sick
jokes.punchline: Reveals Dotcom's disgusting method of getting sick
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor that works because of Kenneth's innocent delivery. Reveals bizarre entourage logic.

jokes.text: But, Liz, I already have. Jack's wrong. No human is truly the master of his fate.
jokes.setup: Liz talking to bag in tree
jokes.punchline: Bag talks back with philosophical response
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment - inanimate object becomes philosophical antagonist. Subverts the self-help narrative perfectly.

jokes.text: the wind rustling against my genitals. That's right, bags have genitals.
jokes.punchline: Reveals bags have genitals
jokes.explanation: Peak absurdist comedy - anthropomorphizing a bag to an uncomfortable extreme. The matter-of-fact delivery sells it.

jokes.text: If you say no, I'll drown myself.
jokes.setup: Asking if people like her hair
jokes.punchline: Threatens suicide over hair compliment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna dramatics - escalates minor validation to life-or-death stakes. Very character-specific narcissism.

jokes.text: Was Dotcom standing that gay?
jokes.punchline: Tracy uses 'gay' as a physical descriptor
jokes.explanation: Dated humor that reveals Tracy's casual homophobia. Works as character flaw but not great comedy by today's standards.

jokes.text: Death to the CIA! Let us all increase production of millet and sunflowers!
jokes.setup: Flashback to Tracy's North Korean propaganda film
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous communist slogans
jokes.explanation: Good satire of propaganda films. The specific agricultural products make it funnier than generic slogans.

jokes.text: It was either that or play a rapping doorman in a Kate Hudson movie.
jokes.setup: Explaining why he did propaganda film
jokes.punchline: Alternative was equally demeaning role
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of Hollywood's limited roles for Black actors. The specificity of 'rapping doorman' is perfect.

jokes.text: Smooth move, Ferguson.
jokes.punchline: Finally hearing the phrase in context
jokes.explanation: Payoff moment that works both as recreation and meta-commentary on the absurdity.

jokes.text: I get it now! It's so funny! Everyone, laugh! Now, you sons of bitches!
jokes.setup: Finally understanding the 'Smooth move, Ferguson' joke
jokes.punchline: Forces everyone to laugh on command
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy moment - demanding forced laughter when he finally gets a joke. Shows his need to control even spontaneous reactions.

jokes.text: I get it now! It's so funny! Everyone, laugh! Now, you sons of bitches!
jokes.punchline: His forced, desperate reaction proves he still doesn't get it
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Tracy's inability to admit defeat and desperate need for control.

jokes.text: his acting notes are often vague.
jokes.setup: Jack asking about Kim Jong-il's treatment
jokes.punchline: Tracy's biggest complaint is poor directing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant understatement - Tracy treats dictator like a bad director. Perfect character-driven comedy showing his priorities.

jokes.text: I'm a parrot.
jokes.setup: Explaining his sex life to comfort himself
jokes.punchline: Reveals he role-plays as a parrot
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy of cringe - two words reveal bizarre sexual dynamic. Jack's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: She pays $1,000 an hour to do that with her trainer.
jokes.setup: Avery breaking concrete blocks in prison
jokes.punchline: It's her expensive workout routine
jokes.explanation: Smart joke about wealthy lifestyle. Prison labor becomes expensive fitness trend. Shows Jack's complete detachment.

jokes.text: Well, look what the cat dragged in.
jokes.setup: Jack calling his ex-girlfriend for help
jokes.punchline: Secretary of State uses casual dismissive greeting
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing their personal relationship dynamic. Secretary of State acting like scorned girlfriend.

jokes.text: 'Mars Attacks!' is awesome!
jokes.setup: Fighting with Jack about her movie taste
jokes.punchline: Secretary of State defends terrible sci-fi movie
jokes.explanation: Good character detail giving Condoleezza Rice terrible movie taste. Humanizes the political figure through bad pop culture.

jokes.text: I'm gonna hang you in my kitchen and fill you with other bags! You will eat your family!
jokes.punchline: Describes cannibalistic bag scenario
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation of Liz's breakdown. The specific threat of bag cannibalism is brilliantly demented.

jokes.text: I told them it was my razor. I have to shave my legs with it, but they have an app for that.
jokes.punchline: Uses iPhone as razor, claims there's an app
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic - 'there's an app for that' taken to ridiculous extreme. Shows Avery's resourcefulness and humor.

jokes.text: Did you also call Sally Ride and her sister?
jokes.punchline: Suggests calling other accomplished women sarcastically
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic response showing Avery's competitiveness with other accomplished women. Character-specific jealousy.

jokes.text: There, are you happy? You dropped a bomb, K! Continue!
jokes.setup: Kenneth admits the truth about missing Tracy
jokes.punchline: Tracy treats emotional revelation like TV drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy response - turns genuine emotion into entertainment direction. Shows his inability to handle real feelings.

jokes.text: 'Smooth move, Ferguson' was just trying to fill a void because Mr. Slattery's political cartoons weren't cutting it. I can't draw hands.
jokes.punchline: Grizz's specific artistic limitation
jokes.explanation: Good character detail that explains the void while giving Grizz a specific flaw.

jokes.text: No matter how much the gate is strait or who punishes the scrolls, I am the captain of my holes.
jokes.setup: Liz trying to recite Invictus inspirationally
jokes.punchline: Completely mangles the famous poem
jokes.explanation: Perfect Liz incompetence with high culture. 'Captain of my holes' is hilariously wrong. Good callback structure.

jokes.text: Avery is now married to Kim Jong-il's son, Kim Jong-un.
jokes.setup: Updates on Avery's situation in North Korea
jokes.punchline: She's been forced into marriage with dictator's son
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation that completely changes the stakes. Turns personal crisis into international incident. Good shock value.

jokes.text: Mortality! Smooth move, Ferguson.
jokes.punchline: Bag invokes mortality while someone says the running gag
jokes.explanation: Surreal ending that ties together the philosophical bag and the running gag. Effective if confusing conclusion.

jokes.text: Put that coffee down. Coffee's for closers only.
jokes.punchline: Glengarry Glen Ross quotes
jokes.explanation: Random pop culture quotes without context. Feels like filler or deleted scene material. Lacks the show's usual cleverness.
